specificity.note: Names concrete figures ($2.45B 2024 base, 21.23% vs 9.7% CAGRs, static 55% share, €495M ceiling, €320M curated, 240–520 band), sources (BIS/R&M, GVR, IndexBox), a quantified perturbation (tamBase→265), and dated report vintages for monitoring.

nonObviousness.note: Both CAGRs are already in the ledger, but the risk supplies a genuinely NEW derived claim — the ratio between them arithmetically forces ~30%+ growth into the excluded PSU/shelf component, decaying the static 55% share — and whyMissable credibly explains why the cross-check reads as belt-and-braces. A rigorous founder cross-auditing the two source scopes could plausibly find it, so not a 4.

mechanismDepth.note: Three-step chain with refs at every arrow and a sound arithmetic core (growth gap must be absorbed by the non-PDU component). Falls short of 4 because the likelihood rationale itself concedes the load-bearing alternative — BIS's 21.23% being an inflated forecast rather than a substitution signal — and the scope mismatch between series (global AI-cluster PSU vs Europe combined) weakens the reconciliation step under hostile questioning.

evidenceQuality.note: Three attached sources plus a fourth stance triangulate the growth gap, and the falsifier search honestly failed to falsify (IndexBox 5–8% confirms the low rack-PDU series; QY Research 26.5% confirms fast-growing substitutes). Not a 4 because the corroborating series are not scope-identical (own admission: 'not scope-identical', global vs Europe, 'panels' vs PDUs) and the actual PDU share decay rate is uncomputed.

decisionRelevance.note: Actionable within a quarter — a named $4,000 IndexBox purchase resolves the key uncertainty, a cheap time-varying-share model change is specified, and indicators (Vertiv/Delta segment mix, 2026 report vintages) have explicit thresholds. Held from 4 because the impact is on sizing credibility rather than a change to operational sequencing or major spend.
